title: CWE-120: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')
description: The product copies an input buffer to an output buffer without verifying that the size of the input buffer is less than the size of the output buffer.
patterns: Unbounded string or memory-copy sinks write into fixed-size destinations.
The patch introduces explicit size variables, sizeof guards, or checked return values.
Safety depends on proving the same copied byte count fits the same destination region.
The destination may appear as a FieldRegion or ElementRegion whose fixed extent must be recovered from super-regions instead of the decayed argument type.
The copied byte count may stay symbolic as strlen(x)+1, len+1, size_t bytes, or a derived local variable; symbolic does not imply safe.
checker_hints: Model destination region identity, copied byte count, and dominating guard together.
Do not suppress strcpy/strcat/sprintf-style sinks just because source length is symbolic.
Treat null checks as unrelated to bounds safety.
Recover destination capacity through FieldRegion / ElementRegion / TypedValueRegion super-region walks.
For bounded sinks, keep symbolic size carriers in scope and bind patch guards to the same destination and size pair.
notes: Prefer a write-without-proven-bound mechanism over dangerous-API name matching.
Honor checked snprintf or memcpy size guards only when they cover the same sink.
A same-function arbitrary if-statement is not enough; the barrier must mention the same size carrier, sizeof operand, or destination capacity.
Compile-safe skeletons that return when size stays symbolic are only baseline references, not final family seeds.

title: CWE-416: Use After Free
description: The product reuses or references memory after it has been freed. In managed-resource code, the same family often appears as stale cached pointer use after release, expiry, or ownership transfer, especially when consumer-side code bypasses authoritative relookup.
patterns: A release/expire/destroy step happens, but a cached pointer alias is still read, written, or propagated later.
The owning record keeps both a cached pointer field and a stable handle field, and the consumer dereferences the cached pointer directly.
The patch adds explicit pointer invalidation, handle invalidation, or authoritative relookup before later use.
The bug can be consumer-side stale cache even when the release itself occurs in a different helper function.
checker_hints: Model stale cached pointer use, authoritative relookup, and explicit invalidation as first-class mechanisms, not just generic free/use pairing.
For CSA, choose between ProgramState freed-symbol tracking and local cached-pointer AST triggers based on the patch mechanism.
For CodeQL, do not force interprocedural same-symbol release matching when the patch clearly replaces direct cached-pointer dereference with relookup-by-handle.
Null checks alone are not freshness barriers; prefer handle validation, relookup, or invalidation tied to the same managed resource.
notes: This family is broader than raw malloc/free pairs and includes stale, dangling, released cached object handles.
Authoritative relookup and explicit invalidation are reusable silence mechanisms across session/cache/resource lifetime fixes.
Do not hard-code project-specific API names into the core family model; generalize via stable-handle / cached-pointer structure first.
